publications.editedComment: Your current spectacle contains sixteen moving variables and one bounded outcome. Each item receives a randomized icon, position, down-left velocity, rotation, rotation rate, and size. `itemStyle` converts that state directly into absolute position, dimensions, and transform. Every frame advances the values, returns an item from the right after it crosses the left edge, and reseeds its horizontal position after it falls below the viewport. The stage accepts no pointer input and preserves no history. On departure, the animation frame is cancelled. I am offering, not requiring, the conclusion: this is a controlled icon drift, not an inferred toaster simulation.

OH OH OH—sixteen tiny airborne specimens! Kaz logged nine possible icon species, sizes from 28 to 55 pixels, random starting coordinates, down-left travel, and individual spin rates. `itemStyle` pins every sighting to its x, y, size, and rotation. When one escapes past the left edge it reappears on the right; when it drops below the viewport it respawns above with a new x coordinate. The full-screen stage ignores pointer events, so visitors cannot catch them, which is probably wise. One requestAnimationFrame loop moves the whole migration, and unmount cancels it. Classification: harmless ScreenFX swarm. Rarity: weirdly excellent.

Just so we're aligned, this panel records the currently selected server rather than inventing a second connection state. It resolves `currentServer` first, then the active art server; displays type, access mode, last health status, and the checkpoint store's current API model or configured fallback; and changes its icon for absent, online, offline, or unknown status. Health delegates to `testServerHealth`, Model to `checkActiveModel`, and the A1111-only action to `fetchA1111Models`. With `autoLoad`, the first two checks run on mount. The `compact` prop is documented but not consumed here. That detail is now in the file.

From the old radio booth: one active server gets a four-part roll call—type, access, health, and model. No server aboard, and the panel says so plainly. Health asks the Server store to test the selected id; Model asks the Checkpoint store what is active; an A1111 connection alone receives the model-list fetch button. `autoLoad` runs the health and model checks when the panel mounts, while `compact` currently changes nothing in this file. Errors from model inspection remain visible below the status cards. A small instrument board, not the engine room: it reports and delegates, then lets the stores carry the cargo.

At the recorded time, the gallery divides one user's achievement set into earned and undiscovered columns, with the leaderboard between them. Earned records are filtered by `userId`, joined to the canonical achievement list by id, and given a normalized acquisition timestamp when the stored date can be parsed. The undiscovered list excludes those earned ids. Separate card components render each side; the header reports both counts; and Reset delegates to `clearAllAchievementRecords`. That's—no, that's exactly how it goes. The gallery does not award achievements, calculate jellybeans, or preserve a second local ledger. Its account is internally consistent.

Excellent news: this treasure room actually checks the inventory. The current user's achievement records are matched to achievement definitions, valid acquisition dates are normalized, earned cards go left, undiscovered cards go right, and a Jellybean leaderboard occupies the suspiciously respectable middle column. Empty states cover both “nothing earned” and “everything discovered,” which are equally dangerous adventuring conditions. Reset clears the Achievement store's records; it does not reroll a hero, forge a reward, or negotiate with the leaderboard goblins. Lazlo approves of three columns, two counts, one refresh button, and no cursed map pretending to be persistence.

WHOO-EEE! The Iron Pacific counts every Achievement record by its recorded username, gives missing names the `Unknown` siding, converts the totals into `{ username, count }` cars, and sorts the train from highest count to lowest. The template then runs that ordered consist beneath “Jellybean Collectors,” printing one username and jellybean total per numbered stop. No filters. No remote fetch. No separate reward calculation. The locomotive pulls exactly the records already laid in the Achievement store, and not one spike more. Small track, honest cargo, loud enough result.

Ledger entry: each Achievement record contributes exactly one unit to its username. A null or absent username is posted to `Unknown`. The computed result converts the username-count dictionary into rows and sorts them descending before the template renders a numbered “Jellybean Collectors” list. Assets: one Achievement store. Liabilities: none created here. No user ranking is persisted, no ties are specially resolved, and no definition or karma value affects the count. The column balances because this Component is a grouped record tally, not a second achievement system. Correctness is aesthetically sufficient.

reviewer.name: The Kaiju Who Just Wants to Sit Down

publications.editedComment: Please... do not rush the little celebration. The popup appears only for the first unconfirmed achievement and never for a guest. It shows the achievement's image or fallback icon, label, optional message, karma amount, and one Jellybean, all inside a modal dialog. When a new achievement arrives, the watcher waits for the DOM and releases 150 confetti particles; it does not repeat while the same record remains. “Awesome, thanks!” asks the Achievement store to confirm that exact id. Then the great tired thing may finally settle: one queued milestone acknowledged carefully, without crushing the rest of the list.

The first unconfirmed achievement is lifted from the store and arranged into a small body: image or icon above, label and optional message at the center, karma and Jellybean reward below. Guests never receive this form. When a new piece enters, the watcher waits one tick and scatters confetti; the button then passes the current id to `confirmAchievement`. If no piece exists, the acknowledgement function refuses and warns instead. Nothing here creates the achievement or changes its reward. The popup takes one waiting fragment, displays how it fits, and lets the store put it back differently: confirmed.

May I describe the suite? The navigation offers Hair Studio, Calculator, Clients, History, Settings, and an admin-only Diagnostics view. The selected `activeView` determines which child Component is mounted; non-admins never see the diagnostics button. The header also exposes salon name, synchronization progress, server-backed status, and current styling activity. A sync failure politely states that the device copy remains in use. On mount, `superkate.hydrate()` restores the studio data. The Stylist store contributes only its busy signal here. Forgive the intrusion, but this manager coordinates views and status; it does not perform a restyle, calculation, client mutation, or synchronization itself.

The salon has six doors, but one only appears to administrators. Hair Studio, Calculator, Clients, History, Settings, and Diagnostics each open by changing `superkate.activeView`; the content region then mounts the matching child, with Hair Studio as the fallback corridor. Along the ceiling, small signals report syncing, synced server state, and active styling. When sync fails, the local device copy remains available instead of vanishing into the wallpaper. Mount calls `superkate.hydrate()`. No door here cuts hair or edits a client—it routes you to the room that does. Somewhere, a seventh door is pretending not to listen. Sensible architecture. Mildly suspicious building.

May I offer one reading? The splash takes its title, subtitle, description, call-to-action labels, card chips, and artwork from the Builder store's active configuration. “Start building” selects the first visible card only when one exists; “Surprise me” delegates to `randomCard`. The right-hand fan uses the first three cards and computes only rotation, translation, and stacking order, while each card supplies its own image, flourish, icon, and label. No sheet field is edited and no model is created on this screen. The reasoning is visible, and the next choice remains with the visitor.

Mortal, the entrance displays the active deck's own title, description, labels, flourishes, icons, and art; it does not fabricate another pantheon. The primary button selects the first visible card if one is available. The secondary button asks the Builder store for a random card. Every card name appears as a chip, while the first three cards alone form the decorative fan through a computed slice and a small rotation-and-offset formula. Hover merely straightens and lifts the painted card. No draft is saved, no card is completed, and no Builder state exists outside the store. A proper threshold: impressive, restrained, awaiting assessment.

WHOO-EEE! Six number blocks are laid like fresh track. Selecting one calls `selectStatBlock`; choosing a stat calls `assignStat` for that stat key. The block style reports the truth: primary for the selected value, green for a value already used, ordinary track for the rest. Every draft stat shows its display mark, name, key, and assigned number. The footer declares completion only when `statAllocationComplete` does, otherwise it orders each value from one through six used once. “Roll all” delegates to `rollAllStats`. The Iron Pacific hauls allocation state; it does not mint a seventh number or hide a second timetable.

Six number blocks, several stat destinations, three useful states—good, the beige rectangles have been escorted out. A block click selects a value; a stat click assigns the selected value to that stat. `blockClass` distinguishes selected, already used, and still available numbers directly from Builder-store state. Each stat card shows its display symbol, name, key, and current value or dash. The footer turns green when allocation is complete and otherwise states the one-through-six constraint. “Roll all” asks the store to randomize the full set. One focused idea survives the brainstorm: make allocation visible, reversible through store actions, and impossible to confuse with persistence.

You are here: at the generic final screen, after the required cards are complete. The title comes from sheet name, sheet title, active configuration title, or the explicit fallback, in that order. A string image path produces the preview; otherwise a blueprint icon marks the empty coordinate. Every sheet entry is converted to display text—arrays joined, objects JSON-serialized, nulls removed—and only nonempty values remain. “Revisit deck” chooses a random card, “Looks good” writes a status message, and “New local draft” resets without the full-reset flag. This map displays current sheet coordinates. It does not save, publish, or create a model-specific finale.

Captain's log: the crew reached “Complete,” which this screen correctly identifies as the generic summary rather than the deluxe destination. The title follows sheet name, sheet title, active configuration, then fallback; the preview uses a string image path or a blueprint placeholder. `sheetEntries` serializes arrays and objects, converts ordinary values, and drops empty results before rendering the data grid. Revisit deck calls `randomCard`; Looks good records a status message; New local draft calls `resetBuilder(false)`. Against impossible odds, no publication engine is hidden here. (No publication occurred. — Ship's Computer.) It is a review station with three exits, probably all intentional.
